chunk_summary: Guidelines for safe feeding and texture modification in children with neurodisability or oral-motor dysfunction to manage moderate acute malnutrition, emphasizing posture, pacing, and energy-dense foods while avoiding aspiration risks.
learning_objectives: Identify signs of feeding and swallowing difficulties in children with neurodisability or oral-motor dysfunction.
Understand safe feeding practices, including texture modification and supportive positioning.
Learn strategies to concentrate calories in safe food textures for nutrition rehabilitation.
Recognize when to escalate care to multidisciplinary services for specialist evaluation.
key_facts: Meals exceeding 30 minutes, coughing, choking, or recurrent chest infections may indicate feeding difficulties.
Aspiration risk and feeding fatigue can contribute to weight faltering in affected children.
Safe feeding practices include smaller, frequent feeds, thicker textures, slow pacing, and upright positioning.
Energy-dense foods like thick khichdi with ghee, curd-based preparations, and nut pastes can aid nutrition rehabilitation.
Early referral to RBSK services and DEIC for multidisciplinary assessment is recommended in India.
reference_quote: Suspect feeding or swallowing difficulty when meals routinely exceed 30 minutes, the child coughs or chokes with liquids, has a 'wet' voice after feeds, drools excessively, has recurrent chest infections, or has poor head and trunk control. (NICE NG62)
